metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: EFP Writing 10 focuses on writing grounded in First Peoples worldviews, emphasizing land-based writing, personal narrative, and community-connected storytelling. Students explore how Indigenous writers craft narratives that honor relationships with land, family, and community while addressing contemporary issues. Through various writing forms including personal essays, creative narratives, and reflective pieces, students develop voice and perspective informed by Indigenous values. This course emphasizes writing as a tool for self-expression, cultural affirmation, and community engagement.
metadata.general_description: An exploration of writing practices grounded in First Peoples worldviews, focusing on land-based narratives, personal storytelling, and community connection.

big_ideas.statement: Indigenous writing emerges from and reflects relationship with land, expressing place-based knowledge, reciprocal responsibilities, and spiritual connection.
big_ideas.elaboration: Land-based writing centers specific geographies, acknowledges the sovereignty and agency of non-human beings, and conveys traditional ecological knowledge. Indigenous writers use their work to affirm continued relationships with ancestral territories, resist erasure, and assert Indigenous presence in place despite colonial displacement and environmental degradation.

big_ideas.statement: Personal narrative and storytelling are powerful vehicles for Indigenous writers to assert identity, process experiences, and contribute to collective cultural narratives.
big_ideas.elaboration: Through writing personal stories, Indigenous authors claim authority over their own narratives, counter official histories, and create records of lived experience. Personal narratives in Indigenous writing often connect individual experience to collective community history, family legacy, and cultural continuity, emphasizing interconnection rather than isolation.

big_ideas.statement: Writing from Indigenous perspectives involves distinctive narrative choices, voice development, and attention to cultural protocols and audience awareness.
big_ideas.elaboration: Indigenous writers make intentional choices about narrative structure, point of view, language, imagery, and tone that reflect Indigenous worldviews and communication styles. Authentic Indigenous voice emerges from cultural grounding, personal experience, and awareness of responsibility to community and cultural knowledge.

big_ideas.statement: Community-centered writing connects individual expression to collective wellbeing, serving purposes of education, healing, activism, and cultural preservation.
big_ideas.elaboration: Indigenous writers often write with awareness of community audience and purpose, viewing writing as work that contributes to community health, resistance, and renewal. This contrasts with individualistic approaches to writing and positions the writer within networks of relationship and responsibility.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect
categories.raw: Students read and analyze Indigenous writing, understanding how writers craft meaning through cultural perspective, voice, and purpose.

competencies.raw: Analyze how Indigenous writers use voice, imagery, and narrative technique to express distinctive perspectives and worldviews
i_can_statements: I can identify distinctive voice and style in Indigenous writing and describe what makes it authentic to Indigenous perspectives
I can analyze how Indigenous writers use imagery related to land, place, and natural world
I can explain how narrative structure and point of view in Indigenous writing reflect cultural values and worldviews
I can discuss how Indigenous writers make intentional choices about language, tone, and tone to convey meaning

competencies.raw: Understand how Indigenous writers address themes of identity, belonging, land connection, and community responsibility
i_can_statements: I can identify themes related to Indigenous identity, cultural continuity, and resilience in Indigenous writing
I can explain how Indigenous writers express relationships with land, place, and non-human beings
I can analyze how Indigenous writers address community concerns and contribute to collective wellbeing through their work
I can discuss how personal narratives in Indigenous writing connect to larger historical and cultural contexts

competencies.raw: Make connections between Indigenous writing and personal experiences, recognizing how reading and writing contribute to identity development and community understanding
i_can_statements: I can relate themes and experiences in Indigenous writing to my own life and identity
I can reflect on how reading Indigenous writing deepens my understanding of First Peoples perspectives and experiences
I can identify writing that speaks to community concerns and social justice issues
I can consider how Indigenous writing models for my own writing and self-expression

categories.name: Create and Communicate
categories.raw: Students develop their own writing grounded in Indigenous perspectives, voice, and purposes, crafting narratives that connect to land, community, and identity.

competencies.raw: Write personal narratives and stories that explore identity, relationships, and experiences from authentic cultural perspective
i_can_statements: I can write personal essays or narratives that explore my identity, family history, and cultural connections
I can craft stories that express my perspectives and experiences authentically
I can develop characters and situations that reflect Indigenous values and relationships
I can revise and refine my writing to strengthen voice, clarity, and impact

competencies.raw: Create writing that expresses connection to land, place, and natural world, drawing on place-based knowledge and observation
i_can_statements: I can write pieces that express my relationship with specific places and lands
I can incorporate detailed observation and sensory imagery in my land-based writing
I can express ecological knowledge, stewardship values, or environmental concerns through writing
I can craft poetry or creative prose that celebrates and honors land relationships

competencies.raw: Develop writing that serves community purposes such as education, advocacy, or cultural preservation
i_can_statements: I can write with awareness of community audience and intended impact
I can create educational writing or resources that share Indigenous knowledge and perspectives
I can write advocacy pieces or letters addressing community concerns and justice issues
I can articulate how my writing contributes to community wellbeing and cultural continuity

areas.name: Indigenous Personal Narrative and Memoir
areas.raw: Exploration of personal storytelling and memoir writing by Indigenous authors, examining how writers claim narrative authority and connect personal experience to cultural history
topics: Family stories and intergenerational narratives
Coming-of-age stories and identity development narratives
Narratives addressing trauma, resilience, and healing
Life writing and autobiographical forms in Indigenous literature
Connecting personal experience to collective histories and cultural contexts
Voice development and authenticity in personal writing

areas.name: Land-Based and Place-Based Writing
areas.raw: Study of writing that centers relationships with specific places and lands, expressing ecological knowledge, stewardship, and sacred geography
topics: Writing about specific territories and ancestral homelands
Sensory observation and detailed place description
Ecological knowledge and environmental stewardship expressed through writing
Sacred geography and spiritual connections to place in Indigenous writing
Resistance narratives addressing land dispossession and environmental harm
Celebration and affirmation of Indigenous presence in place

areas.name: Indigenous Voice, Style, and Narrative Craft
areas.raw: Development of distinctive voice and understanding of narrative techniques and stylistic choices used in Indigenous writing
topics: Voice development grounded in cultural identity and perspective
Narrative structure and point of view in Indigenous storytelling
Dialogue and authentic representation of community voices
Imagery, symbolism, and figurative language choices
Pacing, tone, and style in Indigenous writing
Revision and craft development for strengthening writing

areas.name: Community-Connected Writing and Social Justice
areas.raw: Exploration of writing as a tool for education, activism, healing, and community engagement; writing that addresses social justice and cultural preservation
topics: Writing for activism and advocacy around Indigenous rights and justice
Educational writing and knowledge sharing
Healing and therapeutic dimensions of writing
Writing that documents community experiences and histories
Language revitalization and reclamation through writing
Building audience awareness and considering community purpose in writing
